claim: System prompts bloat when capabilities are bolted on incrementally without architectural refactoring; long prompts cause policy conflicts and context pollution that degrade performance
actionable: When system prompts exceed 100 lines or multiple capabilities need addition, refactor into skills instead of appending to the prompt; periodically audit for policy contradictions
source_title: Tool, skill, or subagent? Decomposing an agent that outgrew its prompt

claim: Skills are composable packaged information that Claude pulls into context only when needed, enabling progressive disclosure and reducing context window pollution
actionable: Extract task-specific policies, procedures, and domain knowledge from system prompts into skills; ensure the system prompt contains only universally needed context
source_title: Tool, skill, or subagent? Decomposing an agent that outgrew its prompt

claim: Start agent design with human-like primitives (code execution, file system, web search) and remove or supplement with custom tools only as needed
actionable: When building agents, begin by exposing bash, file I/O, and web search; only create domain-specific tools when these primitives cannot solve the task efficiently
source_title: Tool, skill, or subagent? Decomposing an agent that outgrew its prompt

claim: Code execution is more efficient than embedding large CSVs in context; agents using code to process data consume 10x fewer tokens than those working with raw data in context
actionable: For data-heavy tasks, give agents code execution capability to write and run Python scripts rather than loading full datasets into the context window
source_title: Tool, skill, or subagent? Decomposing an agent that outgrew its prompt

claim: Sub-agents are most effective for deep research tasks requiring parallelization or for fresh-mind code review where different Claude instances should not share context
actionable: Use sub-agents for: (1) parallelizing independent tasks to reduce latency, or (2) critical review/validation where a separate instance without prior context is needed
source_title: Tool, skill, or subagent? Decomposing an agent that outgrew its prompt

claim: Claude Managed Agents provide native sub-agent observability and logging, solving the communication breakdown and context loss problems of custom sub-agent implementations
actionable: When using sub-agents, deploy via Claude Managed Agents API instead of custom wrappers to ensure proper logging, observability, and communication tracking
source_title: Tool, skill, or subagent? Decomposing an agent that outgrew its prompt

claim: Multi-turn eval failures from policy conflicts can be diagnosed by running structured evals that measure efficiency, turn count, latency, and token usage alongside output quality
actionable: Build eval suites that capture both deterministic metrics (turn count, latency, tokens) and LLM-as-judge grading; run evals before and after architecture changes
source_title: Tool, skill, or subagent? Decomposing an agent that outgrew its prompt

claim: Hill-climbing via iterative eval runs is the standard practice for agent improvement: establish baseline, identify architectural issues, refactor, re-eval, repeat
actionable: After each architectural change to an agent, run the full eval suite and compare metrics; only commit changes that improve target metrics without regressions
source_title: Tool, skill, or subagent? Decomposing an agent that outgrew its prompt

claim: Frontier models are intelligent enough to eliminate many sub-agents entirely; consolidate multi-agent workflows into a single capable instance unless parallelization or isolation is required
actionable: Before creating a sub-agent, verify that a single agent instance with proper tools and skills cannot handle the task; only add sub-agents if parallelization or isolation provides measurable benefit
source_title: Tool, skill, or subagent? Decomposing an agent that outgrew its prompt
